Word Embeddings

Introduction

Deep neural network models (including LLMs) generally can not process text directly because text data is categorial and therefore it isn't compatible with mathematical operations used to implement and train neural networks. This necessitates the need of representing words as continous-valued vectors. This concept of converting data into vector format is often refered to as embedding.

Through embedding models, we can process data of all types including video, audio, and text. Typically, different data types require different embedding models (i.e. the embedding model for audio would not be suitable for text data), but there are current research that try to design one embedding for all types of data (Girdhar et al., 2023).

Word2Vec

Word vectors

A dense vector for each word, chosen so that it is similar to vectors of words that appear in similar contexts. Word vectors are also called word embedding or word representations.

Word2Vec Algorithm

There are several algorithms and frameworks that have been developed to generate word embeddings. The earliest and most popular method is Word2Vec, proposed by Mikolov et al., 2013. Word2Vec trained a neural network architecture to generate word embeddings by predicting the context of a word given the target word or vice versa. This approach is similar to the notion of "You shall now a word by a company it keeps". The main idea behind Word2Vec is that words that appear in similar contexts tend to have similar meanings. For this reason, when projected into low dimensional word embeddings (2-3 for visualization purposes), we can see that similar terms cluster together. The higher the dimensionality, the more effective the model is in terms of capturing nuances word relationships but this comes with the cost of computation efficiency. For GPT-2 and GPT-3 models, the embedding size varied based on the specific model variant and size. The smaller GPT-2 models uses an embedding that has 768 dimensions, while the largest GPT-3 model uses an embedding size of 12228 dimensions.

We have a large corpus of text, where every word in a fixed vocabulary is represented by a vector. Go through each position in the text, which has a center word and context words .

For each position , predict context words within a window of fixed size , given a center word . Data likelihood:

The objective function is the average negative log likelihood:

The question is how to calculate . The answer is, for the sake of convenience, we'll use two vectors per word , including and . Here, is the word vector for the word when is the center word, and is the word vector when is the context word.

Prediction function

The prediction function has the form of the softmax function, where the numerator term is the dot product that compares similarity of and , where larger dot product means higher probability. The denominator normalizes over the entire vocabulary to give probability distribution.

References

Girdhar, R., El-Nouby, A., Liu, Z., Singh, M., Alwala, K. V., Joulin, A., & Misra, I. (2023). ImageBind One Embedding Space to Bind Them All. 2023 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR). https://doi.org/10.1109/cvpr52729.2023.01457

Mikolov, T., Chen, K., Corrado, G., & Dean, J. (2013). Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space. International Conference on Learning Representations.
